steps.title: A lamp that knows when it's dark

narration.default: Bedside lamps that switch themselves on at sunset. Garden lights that wake up at dusk. Streetlamps that come alive when the sky goes orange. They all share one trick: a tiny sensor that watches the light, and a program that decides when it's dark enough. Today you'll build that exact program. If you haven't done Blink an LED and Button and LED yet, finish those first — this tutorial assumes you've met pinMode, digitalWrite, if-else, and the simulator already.

You've seen this twice now. Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. Setup runs once when the program starts. Loop runs forever, over and over, while the Arduino has power.

Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 9 and mode to output — pin 9 will send voltage out to the LED. Notice we only need pinMode for the LED. The light sensor will go on an analog pin, and analog inputs configure themselves automatically. One pinMode line, that's it.

steps.title: The LDR — what does it report?

narration.default: The light sensor we'll use is called an LDR — short for light-dependent resistor. Now think for a second. A button has two answers: pressed or not pressed. high or low. A neat little yes-or-no. But light isn't a yes-or-no. A room can be pitch dark, or sunny, or anywhere in between — dim, twilight, cloudy afternoon, lamp-lit evening. So when our program asks the LDR what it sees, what could the answer possibly be? high and low aren't enough.

Here's the answer: the LDR reports a number. Not high or low — an actual number from 0 to 1023. 0 means pitch dark. 1023 means full sun. A dim room might be 200. A sunny window might be 900. Anywhere in between, anywhere on the scale. The block that reads this is called analogRead — analog meaning a smooth scale, not just two states. digitalRead gives you one of two answers; analogRead gives you one of 1024. That's the whole mystery solved.

We need somewhere to put the LDR's number so we can use it in the next step. Open the Variables category, click Make a Variable, and name it lightLevel. A variable is just a labelled box — every loop, we'll drop the latest reading into it.

Drag a set-variable block into the loop. Click its dropdown and pick lightLevel — that's the box we just made. Right now the value slot just has a 0 in it; the next step swaps that for the live sensor reading.

steps.title: Drop analogRead into the value slot

narration.default: Now go to the Arduino category and drag an analogRead block. Drop it into the value slot of the set-variable line. Set its pin to A0. Now every loop, the program asks the LDR what it sees and parks that number into the lightLevel box.

Now we have to make a decision. lightLevel is somewhere between 0 and 1023, but the lamp only has two states — on or off. So we have to draw a line. We pick a number — a threshold — and say: below this number, it's dark, switch the lamp on; above this number, it's bright, switch it off. We'll start with 500 — roughly the middle of the scale. Below 500 means dark, lamp on. This pattern — pick a threshold, compare against it — is one of the most common shapes in all of programming.

Same if-else block you used in Button and LED. Open the Control category and drag an if-else into the loop, just below the read-LDR step. The diamond slot is where our question goes. The if branch runs when the answer is yes, the else branch when the answer is no.

Open the Operators category and drag the less-than block. Drop it into the diamond slot of the if-else. On the left side, drop the lightLevel variable from the Variables category. On the right side, type 500. The question now reads: is lightLevel less than 500? If yes, it's dark. If no, it's bright.

Drag a digitalWrite block into the if branch — the top half. Set pin to 9 and value to high. When lightLevel is below 500, this line runs: 5 volts on pin 9, the LED lights up. The room is dark, the lamp comes on.

Drag another digitalWrite into the else branch — the bottom half. Pin 9, value low. When lightLevel is 500 or above, this line runs instead: 0 volts on pin 9, LED dark. The room is bright, the lamp stays off.

Drag a delay block to the bottom of the loop, after the if-else. Set it to 200 milliseconds. Every loop should have at least a small pause — it stops the Arduino from spinning at full speed for no reason, and 200 milliseconds is fast enough that the lamp still feels instantly responsive when the room dims.

Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose LED. In the pin dialog, choose pin 9 and click Save. The virtual lamp appears next to the virtual Arduino — wired to the same pin your code is writing high and low to.

Click the plus button again and choose Photoresistor — that's the simulator's name for the LDR. Set the pin to A0 and click Save. A new component appears with a slider underneath it. The slider is how you fake a sunrise or a sunset in the simulator: drag it up for bright, drag it down for dark.
Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds. The simulator goes live.

Click and drag the slider under the photoresistor. Push it down — the LED comes on the moment the number passes 500. Push it back up — the LED goes dark again. You're simulating the sun setting, then rising. The same code, the same threshold, every time. You just built a streetlamp.

Here's the honest truth: 500 was a guess. It's halfway between 0 and 1023, which felt reasonable. But your bedroom isn't average — maybe your nights are very dark and 500 makes the lamp turn on too early. Maybe your curtains are thin and morning light leaks in, and 500 makes it turn off too late. In real life you'd test in your actual room and tune the number. A bigger number means the lamp comes on sooner, while there's still some light. A smaller number means the lamp waits for it to get really dark before switching on. The number isn't magic — it's a knob you adjust to your taste.

Try this: change 500 to 200 and run again. The lamp now waits much longer before switching on — only the deepest darkness triggers it. Then try 800 — the lamp comes on almost any time the room dims. Or add a second LED on pin 10, with its own pinMode and its own if-else using a different threshold. Below 700, the dim-light LED comes on. Below 300, both come on — a two-stage night light that gets brighter as the room gets darker.

You learned three big things today. analogRead — the block that reads a sensor on a smooth scale from 0 to 1023, instead of just high or low. The threshold pattern — pick a number, compare against it, decide. And the shape underneath all of this: sensor, decision, output. Read the world. Compare. Act. That same shape runs every thermostat, every soil-moisture irrigation system, every smart streetlamp, every battery-low warning on your phone. Once you see the pattern, you'll spot it everywhere — and you can write it now.
